title: Corned Beef Hash
subtitle: Corned beef and diced potato fried until crusted, with soft onion and a fried egg
excerpt: Corned beef hash is a one-pan dish of shredded corned beef, diced potato, and onion, pressed into a hot pan until it forms a crackling brown crust. The crust is the point. Everything else in the pan is secondary to the effort of building and keeping it.
sourceNotes: British and American tradition. Corned beef hash became a standard British dish in the 20th century, particularly associated with tinned corned beef which was widely available through wartime rationing and beyond. The crust method is documented in British and American hash recipes from the late 19th century.

recipe.temperatureNote: Hob only; no oven temperature required.

glossaryTerms.definition: From the French hacher, meaning to chop. A dish of previously cooked meat and vegetables, roughly chopped and fried together until the base forms a crackling crust. The browned layer that develops on the bottom of the pan is the defining characteristic.

content.text: Corned beef hash uses either tinned corned beef or the shredded remains of a braised beef brisket. The tinned version is quicker and more traditional in the British context; brisket gives a looser, more textured result. Either way, the hash needs a very hot pan, patience, and restraint: do not stir it once it is pressed flat, or the crust will never form.

attrs.title: Corned beef: tinned or braised
attrs.body: Tinned corned beef is the traditional British choice and works well here: break it into rough chunks directly from the tin. If you have leftover braised beef brisket, shred it and use in the same quantity. The braised version gives a slightly more open texture; the tinned version is denser and saltier, so adjust the seasoning accordingly.

items.prepNote: corned beef from a tin, or braised brisket, shredded into rough chunks
peeled and cut into 1 cm dice

content.text: Parboil the {{potato}} of diced potato in salted water for 5 minutes until just starting to soften but still holding its shape. Drain well.
Heat a generous film of oil in a wide frying pan over a medium heat. Add the {{onion}} diced onion and cook for 6 to 8 minutes until soft and lightly golden.

Add the parboiled potato and the {{beef-brisket}} of corned beef to the pan. Add the Worcestershire sauce. Mix everything together briefly, then press down into an even layer with a spatula. Increase the heat to medium-high.
Leave undisturbed for 8 to 10 minutes until a deep brown crust forms on the base. This is the
crust. Scrape and turn sections of the hash over to expose the crust, then press flat again and cook for another 5 minutes to form a second crust. Season well.
Serve directly from the pan with a fried egg on top of each portion, if using.

items.cause: Too much stirring, or the pan was not hot enough.
items.fix: Once the hash is pressed flat, do not touch it for at least 8 minutes. A medium-high heat is needed to form the crust; medium heat will only steam the mixture.

items.cause: Tinned corned beef is already heavily salted.
items.fix: Taste before adding any extra salt. Adjust the Worcestershire sauce accordingly.
The parboil is essential; raw diced potato will not cook through in the time the crust takes to form. Five minutes in salted water until just yielding is the minimum.

content.text: Corned beef hash entered the British kitchen through the widespread availability of tinned corned beef, which became a staple during and after the Second World War. The word corned refers not to maize but to large grains of rock salt historically used to cure the brisket. The hash form, chopped, pressed, and crisped in a pan, is the most practical way to serve it and the form that rewards technique most directly.
